media.alt_text: Hurricane Preparedness. Prepare Before Hurricane Season. Develop an evacuation plan. Assemble disaster supplies: food, water, batteries, charger, radio, cash. Get an insurance checkup and document your possessions. Create a communication plan with a hand-written list of contacts. Strengthen your home.

media.alt_text: Hurricane Preparedness. Understand Forecast Information. Rely on forecasts from the National Hurricane Center & your local NWS office. Know your alerts & the difference between a Watch and Warning. Focus on potential impacts, regardless of storm size or category. Know that deadly hazards occur well outside of the Forecast Cone.

media.alt_text: Hurricane Preparedness - Get Moving When a Storm Threatens. Protect your home: cover windows, secure doors and loose items. Determine sheltering options and consider your pets. Ready your go-bag, meds and supplies, charge phone, fill up/charge vehicle. Help your neighbors, especially the elderly & other vulnerable people. Follow evacuation orders if given.

media.alt_text: Hurricane Preparedness - Stay Protected During Storms. Stay in your safe places from water and wind. Have a way to get weather alerts and forecast updates. Keep in mind that impacts can be felt far from the coast. Listen to local officials and avoid travel unless ordered to evacuate.

media.alt_text: Hurricane Preparedness - Use Caution After Storms. If you evacuated, only return home when directed that it's safe to do so. Remain vigilant, as hazards remain: heat, downed power lines, floodwaters, and more. Clean up safely: don't push yourself, and check on neighbors. Only use generators outdoors, 20+ feet from your home. Prepare for the likelihood that help and communications may not be available.

Clear brush away from your home to create defensible space. Use fire-resistant landscaping. Know your potential evacuation routes. Have an emergency supply kit ready to go.

🔹 Never use it inside your home, basement or garage
🔹 Always use it outside & away from your house
🔹 Keep it dry & properly grounded

media.alt_text: During a flood, water levels and the rate at which the water is flowing can quickly change. Remain aware and monitor local radio and television. 1- Get to higher ground. Get out of the areas subject to flooding. 2- Do not drive into water. Do not drive or walk into flooded areas. It only takes 6" of water to know you off your feet. 3- Stay informed.

media.alt_text:
Graphic provides tips on what to do if a storm has damaged your home/property: 1) Before you clean up, take photos of your damage home and belongings. You do not need to wait for an inspector to view damages before cleaning up. 2) Make a list of damaged/lost items and gather any original receipts you have for those items. 3) Call your insurance agent to submit a claim.
Having this info on hand will help speed up the process with your insurance company and other programs.

tweets.text_resolved: Hawaii + Guam: Tropical storms can be deadly, bringing heavy rain and flash flooding. Get your emergency supplies ready and stay alert for watches/warnings. Never walk/drive into floodwaters: 6" can knock you down, 12" can sweep a car away. https://t.co/VHv9VPDOsB

media.alt_text: Alt text: Tropical Storms can be deadly. Even if it's not a hurricane, tropical storms are still dangerous. They can bring heavy rain, which can lead to deadly flooding. Remain vigilant if a Tropical Storm Watch or Warning is issued for your area. Never walk or drive into floodwaters. It only takes 6 inches to knock you over and only 12 inches to sweep a car away.

media.alt_text:
Winter storm watch: danger is possible. conditions are favorable for a storm, heavy snow, freezing rain and sleet. Issued 12 to 48 hours before.
Winter storm warning: danger is imminent. heavy snow, freezing rain and sleet is occurring or certain. issued 12 to 24 hours before

tweets.text:
How to stay safe and warm if you lose power due to the Nor’easter:
- Stay inside
- Open blinds or curtains during the day and use the sun’s heat to warm your home
- Close vents and doors in unoccupied rooms to keep heat from escaping
https://t.co/PXgtO0Yc7z https://t.co/rBAYzEoAn4

media.alt_text: Illustration showing a closed door with a rolled towel at the bottom and a window with curtains open, with the text "Low-Cost Tips for Keeping Your Home Warm: Close the vents and room doors that are not being used. tweets.text:
Damaging wind, hail & tornadoes are forecast for parts of the southern and central U.S. this week. Be prepared in case of a tornado warning:

- Go to a small interior room like a closet, basement, or storm cellar
- Stay away from windows, large open rooms, doors, or outside walls https://t.co/44bIAsEW6d
tweets.text_resolved:
Damaging wind, hail & tornadoes are forecast for parts of the southern and central U.S. this week. Be prepared in case of a tornado warning:

- Go to a small interior room like a closet, basement, or storm cellar
- Stay away from windows, large open rooms, doors, or outside walls https://t.co/44bIAsEW6d

media.alt_text: Know where to go when sheltering from a tornado. Quickly move to your basement and bring your emergency supply kit. If you do not have a basement, move to an interior room with no windows. Top floors DO NOT protect you. Exterior rooms and rooms with windows DO NOT protect you. NO PLACE OUTSIDE is safe from a tornado.

tweets.text:
Carbon monoxide is an invisible killer. If you were affected by the recent winter storm, stay safe!

• Test CO alarms.
• Make sure vents for the dryer, furnace, stove, and fireplace are clear of snow.
• Use portable generators outdoors, away from doors, windows, and vents. https://t.co/BfCErts1UO

media.alt_text: Alt text: Adding layers will help keep you warm as the temperature drops. When it's chilly, wear 1-2 layers on top including an outer layer. On bottom, wear a long layer and warm shoes. When it's cold, wear 2-3 layers on top, including gloves, a warm hat, and an outer layer. On bottom, wear 1-2 layers, including boots. In extreme cold, wear 3+ layers on top, including a warm hat, face mask, gloves, and an outer layer.

tweets.text: If you are on the East Coast, don't ignore the risks that Atlantic storm activity will have in coastal areas. Offshore storms can still cause life-threatening conditions. If you find yourself stuck in a rip current, know your options. https://t.co/hd3gyjs7oS
tweets.text_resolved: If you are on the East Coast, don't ignore the risks that Atlantic storm activity will have in coastal areas. Offshore storms can still cause life-threatening conditions. If you find yourself stuck in a rip current, know your options. https://t.co/hd3gyjs7oS

media.alt_text:
If caught in a rip current:

Relax, rip currents don't pull you under.
Don't swim against the current.
Swim out of the current, then to shore.
If you can't escape, float or tread water.
If you need help, yell or wave for assistance.

Rip currents are powerful currents of water moving away from shore. They can sweep even the even the strongest swimmer away from shore. If at all possible, swim near a lifeguard.

media.alt_text:
A hurricane watch is issued when hurricane force winds are possible within 48 hours. Prepare your home by boarding up windows and moving loose items indoors. Have an emergency supply kit ready.

A hurricane warning is issued when hurricane force winds are expected within 36 hours. Be ready to seek shelter in a sturdy structure or evacuate if ordered.

media.alt_text: How to survive a rip current. Relax, stay calm and float to conserve energy. Do NOT try to swim directly to shore. Swim along the shoreline until you escape the current's pull. If you feel you can't reach shore, face the shore and call or wave for help.

media.alt_text:
Wildfire burn scars are a flood risk.
before a fire: liter
during a fire: burning vegetation and liter. soil particles covered with water repellent compounds
after a fire: ash and burnt topsoil, water repellent soil 
during heavy rain: ash, water repellent soil, top layer becomes saturated, ash layer slides downhill

media.alt_text: Storm Planning Timeline. If the forecast calls for severe weather, start preparing now: make sure that you have emergency supplies; know your safe places; have a family communications plan. The day before, forecast accuracy continues to improve: adjust plans; make sure your phone can receive WEAs; ensure your shelter is clean and accessible. The day of, remain vigilant and aware of any active Watches. A Warning may be issued at a moment's notice; you may only have seconds to take action!

media.alt_text: Thunderstorm warning: A severe thunderstorm warning is issued when a severe storm is happening or imminent. Take shelter immediately! Stay tuned to forecast updates and monitor sky conditions. Know where to take shelter. Be prepared. Weather.gov/safety/thunderstorm.

- Save receipts and maintain good records

media.alt_text: Common Reasons for Not Getting Approval. Top 5 reasons for not getting approved for FEMA assistance: 1. You have insurance coverage for the loss. 2. Your home is still safe to live in despite the damage. 3. The damaged home was not your primary residence. 4. You received assistance for the same losses or expenses from another source. 5. Inspectors can't reach applicants at the contact information provided.

tweets.text_resolved: West Virginia: Do not attempt to enter flooded roadways closed off by local authorities. It only takes 6 inches of fast-moving floodwaters to knock an adult off their feet and 12 inches can carry away most cars. Turn around, don't drown! https://t.co/jxh0JoNmox

media.alt_text:
Steer clear of flooded roads:
1. Never drive on flooded roads--almost half of flood deaths happen in vehicles.
2. 6 inches of water is enough to cause you to lose control of your vehicle.
3. If you encounter flood waters on a roadway, Turn Around, Don't Drown.

tweets.text_resolved: If you have mobility or medical needs and are in the path of severe storms, ask for assistance from neighbors or family members when preparing your home. It may be more challenging for the elderly or disabled to make sure they are adequately prepared. https://t.co/BpoJ3YSThP

media.alt_text:
Storm headed your way? If you have mobility or medical needs: 
1. Plan ahead for accessible transportation in case you need to evacuate.
2. Ask neighbors for help gathering supplies and to check on you.
3. Have an extra mobility device, such as a cane or walker.
4. Add medical information to your electronic devices.

media.alt_text:
Tornado watch vs warning

Watch: A tornado is possible. Stay tuned to local radio/TV for information. Know where you'll shelter if needed.

Warning: A tornado is happening or imminent. Take shelter immediately.

Evacuate to an area with better air quality if possible.

media.alt_text:
There are a few reasons why you might not be approved for disaster assistance. Below are the top 5 reasons for not getting approved for FEMA assistance:
1. You have insurance coverage for the loss.
2. Your home is still safe to live in despite the damage.
3. The damaged home was not your primary residence. 
4. You received assistance for the same losses or expenses from another source.
5. Inspectors have been unable to reach applicants at the contact information provided.

- If you have insurance, file a claim.
- Apply for FEMA Assistance.
- FEMA inspector will contact you to schedule an inspection.
- Read your FEMA letter carefully.
- Use your money from FEMA for disaster-related purposes.

media.alt_text: Tornado Sheltering Guidelines: Seek the best available area immediately when a Tornado Warning is issued. Worst options: mobile homes, vehicles, underneath a highway overpass. Bad: Large open rooms like gyms, manufactured housing. Good: Interior room or building, basement. Best Options: Above or below ground Tornado Storm Shelter (NSSA/ICC 500 compliant, recommended by FEMA) or a specifically-designed FEMA Safe Room

#DYK you have the right to appeal if you disagree with our decision regarding your disaster assistance application?

When starting your appeal, gather documents to support your case, including bills and repair estimates.

Read our step-by-step guide: http://fema.gov/blog/how-appeal-femas-decision-step-step-guide https://t.co/uGMpPMjNxM

media.alt_text: How to appeal: If you disagree with FEMA's decision, you may appeal. It must be submitted within 60 days of the date of the decision letter. If you choose to appeal, you can submit either a signed letter or appeal form. Both are optional to include with your appeal. Download appeal form: go.dhs.gov/UMd. 3 ways to submit your appeal: online at DisasterAssistance.gov, by fax at 800-827-8112, or by mail. All appeals are reviewed and decisions are usually made within 30 days of receiving the appeal.

media.alt_text: Understanding your FEMA determination letter. If you applied for FEMA assistance you will receive a letter explaining FEMA's decision within ten days after the inspector's visit. If you are approved for assistance, you may get funds by direct deposit or by a U.S. Treasury check based on what you chose during your application. Only use money from FEMA for the reasons described in your approval letter. Keep your records and receipts for at least three years. Learn more: Fema.gov/IA

tornado sheltering guidelines. seek the best available refuge area immediately when a tornado warning is issued. your chance of surviving a tornado is excellent if you follow these guidelines:
worst options: mobile homes, vehicles, underneath a highway overpass
bad options: large open rooms 
good options: interior room of a well-constructed home or building or basement
best options: above or below ground

tweets.text_resolved:
#CaliforniaFires survivors: Once we review your disaster assistance application, you’ll receive a determination letter explaining whether you’re approved for assistance.

If not approved, you have the right to file an appeal within 60 days.

Learn more: http://fema.gov/blog/how-appeal-femas-decision-step-step-guide https://t.co/xYqkR1JBn0

media.alt_text:
How to appeal

If you disagree with FEMA's decision, you may appeal. It must be submitted within 60 days of the date of the decision letter. If you choose to appeal, you can submit either a signed letter or appeal form. Both are optional to include.

After submitting your appeal

All appeals are reviewed and decisions usually are made within 30 days of receiving the appeal, however, it may take up to 90 days for a decision. You will be notified either by mail or DisasterAssistance.gov.

media.alt_text:
Disaster evacuation tips

When & where to go: Leave immediately when told to evacuate by local officials. Check with your local emergency management office for info on evacuation routes.

What to do: Check with neighbors who may need a ride. Take your pets with you. Bring essential supplies like water, food, and medications.

media.alt_text:
Insured? FEMA may still help! 
1. File your insurance claim: contact your insurance company and file your claim ASAP.
2. Send your insurance info to FEMA: share your insurance approval or denial with FEMA. This helps us see how we can assist you further. FEMA cannot duplicate benefits covered under your insurance provider.

tweets.text_resolved:

media.alt_text: California Wildfires: how to help
Donations: the best way is through cash donations to trusted organizations. Cash is efficient, flexible & requires no packaging or transport. Find a trusted organization through NVOAD: nvoad.org
Volunteer: Do NOT self-deploy to disaster areas. Local orgs can help you where volunteers are needed. Provide paid services and goods for disaster relief: CALvoad.org

Volunteer: keep in mind that recovery lasts longer than the media attention. There will be volunteer needs for many months, often many years, after the disaster. Your help is needed long after a disaster.

author.description: This channel provides information about FEMA's mission. For emergencies, call the fire department, your emergency management agency, or 9-1-1.

tweets.text:
Los Angeles residents affected by the ongoing wildfires can now apply for federal disaster assistance. 

Three easy ways to apply:
- Online: https://t.co/gryh5A6N9X
- Call: 800-621-3362 (Press 3 for an interpreter who speaks your language)
- FEMA App: https://t.co/bCOlw2Mh7B https://t.co/TS5QSv6PEE
tweets.text_resolved:
Los Angeles residents affected by the ongoing wildfires can now apply for federal disaster assistance. 

Three easy ways to apply:
- Online: http://DisasterAssistance.gov
- Call: 800-621-3362 (Press 3 for an interpreter who speaks your language)
- FEMA App: http://fema.gov/app https://t.co/TS5QSv6PEE

tweets.text:
We urge residents to not return home until local officials have confirmed it's safe to do so.

Even if the fire has passed, there may still be hidden dangers such as poor air quality, unstable structures, and downed power lines.

Stay informed and prioritize your safety. https://t.co/JOy7KuqGLc
tweets.text_resolved:
We urge residents to not return home until local officials have confirmed it's safe to do so.

Even if the fire has passed, there may still be hidden dangers such as poor air quality, unstable structures, and downed power lines.

Stay informed and prioritize your safety. https://t.co/JOy7KuqGLc

media.alt_text:
Do my state and local officials say it's safe?
Does my neighborhood have power?
Is there a safe route home?
Will I be able to communicate once I am there?
Can I access food, water, and healthcare?
Do I have a backup place to stay?

If not, don't go.

media.alt_text: California Wildfires: Evacuation & Shelter Information

Be prepared to evacuate!
Have different ways to receive emergency alerts and follow evacuation orders from local officials.
If told to evacuate, do it immediately! Your life depends on it.

Know where to go:
- Text SHELTER and your ZIP code to 43362.
- Visit RedCross.org/Shelters to find open shelters near you.
Find real time evacuation & shelter information: LAcounty.gov/emergency.

media.alt_text: California Wildfires: State & Local Resources

Follow local officials for the latest information.
LA County on X: @CountyofLA
LA County Office of Emergency Management on X: @ReadyLACounty
LA County Fire Department on X: @LACoFDPIO
LA County Sheriff on X: @LASDHQ
Call 1-800-854-7771 for 24/7 mental health support through the LA County Department of Mental Health.
Call 211 or visit 211CA.org for free, confidential service to find local resources 24/7.

🔗 https://t.co/7CEKbs3xbq https://t.co/N6xOAqtkRX
tweets.text_resolved:
We join the nation in mourning the passing of President Jimmy Carter. At FEMA, he will be remembered not only for his service to our nation, but also as our agency's founder. His commitment to service strengthened our ability to address disasters.

🔗 http://fema.gov/press-release/20250108/fema-mourns-agencys-founder-president-jimmy-carter https://t.co/N6xOAqtkRX

media.alt_text: "President Carter possessed the foresight to recognize the nation required a unified and coordinated emergency response system. Much like he lived his life in service to others, he laid the groundwork for generations of emergency managers dedicated to putting people first."-- FEMA Administrator Deanne Criswell

media.alt_text: You can find shelter locations by texting. Text "Shelter" and your ZIP code to 43362. Usable anytime cell connection is available, even during a blackout. Easy to share results with family and friends via test. State and local officials may be aware of additional shelters not on the texting feature. Standard carrier fees apply, no additional cost to use this service.
Information in Spanish
